questions.subject: English
questions.examName: English and Logical Reasoning Prep Guide
questions.q:
Choose the word that is most opposite in meaning to the given word:
Iconoclast
questions.solution:
Iconoclast (noun) : If you describe someone as an iconoclast, you mean that they often criticize beliefs and things that are generally accepted by society. 
 
Adherent (noun) : An adherent is someone who holds a particular belief or supports a particular person or group. = follower, supporter

Ex. This idea is gaining adherents.
 
Demagogue (noun) : If you say that someone such as a politician is a demagogue you are criticizing them because you think they try to win people's support by appealing to their emotions rather than using reasonable arguments.
 
Saboteur (noun) : A saboteur is a person who deliberately damages or destroys things such as machines, railway lines, and bridges in order to weaken an enemy or to make a protest. In Britain, people who try to stop blood sports such as fox hunting are also referred to as saboteurs.

Ex. The saboteurs had planned to bomb buses and offices.
 
Elite (noun) : You can refer to the most powerful, rich, or talented people within a particular group, place, or society as the elite.

Ex. ...a government comprised mainly of the elite...
Ex. We have a political elite in this country.
questions.source: English and Logical Reasoning Prep Guide

questions.subject: English
questions.examName: English and Logical Reasoning Prep Guide
questions.q:
Choose the word that is most opposite in meaning to the given word:
Baleful
questions.solution:
Baleful (adj) : 
Having a harmful or destructive effect.

Salacious (adj) :
If you describe something such as a book or joke as salacious, you think that it deals with sexual matters in an unnecessarily detailed way.
Ex. The newspapers once again filled their columns with salacious details.

Gregarious (adj) :
Someone who is gregarious enjoys being with other people.
Ex. She is a gregarious and outgoing lady.

Sinister (adj) :
Somthing that is sinister seems evil or harmful.
Ex. A sinister smile.

Beneficent (adj)
1. (of a person) generous or doing good.
2. Resulting in good
questions.source: English and Logical Reasoning Prep Guide

questions.subject: English
questions.examName: English and Logical Reasoning Prep Guide
questions.q:
Choose the word that is most opposite in meaning to the given word:
Circumspect
questions.solution:
Circumspect (adj) :
If you are circumspect, you are cautious in what you do and say and do not take risks. 
Ex. The banks should have been more circumspect in their dealings.
 
Griveous (adj) :
If you describe something such as a loss as grievous, you mean that it is extremely serious or worrying in its effects.
Ex. Their loss would be a grievous blow to our engineering industries.
 
Bumptious (adj) :
If you say that someone is bumptious, you are criticizing them because they are very pleased with themselves and their opinions.
 
Reckless (adj) :
If you say that someone is reckless, you mean that they act in a way which shows that they do not care about danger or the effect their behaviour will have on other people.
Ex. He is charged with causing death by reckless driving.
 
Sanguine (adj) :
If you are sanguine about something, you are cheerful and confident that things will happen in the way you want them to.
Ex. He's remarkably sanguine about the problems involved.
questions.source: English and Logical Reasoning Prep Guide

questions.subject: English
questions.examName: English and Logical Reasoning Prep Guide
questions.q:
Choose the word that is most opposite in meaning to the given word:
Dejected
questions.solution:
Dejected (adj) :
If you are dejected, you feel miserable or unhappy, especially because you have just been disappointed by something.
Ex. Everyone has days when they feel dejected or down.
    
Despondent (adj) :
If you are despondent, you are very unhappy because you have been experiencing difficulties that you think you will not be able to overcome.
Ex. I feel despondent when my work is rejected.
 
Jubilant (adj) :
If you are jubilant, you feel extremely happy because of a success.
Ex. Virat Kohli was jubilant after making an impressive comeback from a month on the injured list.
 
Lacklustre (adj) :
If you describe something or someone as lacklustre, you mean that they are not exciting or energetic.
Ex. He has already been blamed for his party's lackluster performance during the election campaign.
 
Menial (adj) :
Menial work is very boring, and the people who do it have a low status and are usually badly paid.
Ex. ...low paid menial jobs, such as cleaning and domestic work.
questions.source: English and Logical Reasoning Prep Guide

questions.subject: English
questions.examName: English and Logical Reasoning Prep Guide
questions.q:
Choose the word that is most opposite in meaning to the given word:
Feckless
questions.solution:
Feckless (adj) :
If you describe someone as feckless, you mean that they lack determination or strength, and are unable to do anything properly; incompetent
Ex: He regarded the young man as feckless and irresponsible.
 
Jaded (adj) : If you are jaded, you feel bored, tired, and not enthusiastic, for example because you have had too much of the same thing; bored
 
Morbid (adj) : If you describe a person or their interest in something as morbid, you mean that they are very interested in unpleasant things, especially death, and you think this is strange.
Ex: Some people have a morbid fascination with crime.
 
Competent (adj) : Someone who is competent is efficient and effective.
Ex: He was a loyal, distinguished and very competent civil servant.
Ex: ...a competent performance.
 
Forlorn (adj) : If someone is forlorn, they feel alone and unhappy.
Ex: One of the demonstrators, a young woman, sat forlorn on the pavement...
questions.source: English and Logical Reasoning Prep Guide

questions.subject: English
questions.examName: English and Logical Reasoning Prep Guide
questions.q:
Choose the word that is most opposite in meaning to the given word:
Denounce
questions.solution:
Denounce (verb) :
If you denounce a person or an action, you criticize them severely and publicly because you feel strongly that they are wrong or evil.
Ex. German leaders denounced the attacks and pleaded for tolerance.
Ex. Some 25,000 demonstrators denounced him as a traitor.
 
Convict (verb) :
If someone is convicted of a crime, they are found guilty of that crime in a law court.
Ex. In 1977 he was convicted of murder and sentenced to life imprisonment.
Ex. There was insufficient evidence to convict him.
 
Delineate (verb) :
If you delineate something such as an idea or situation, you describe it or define it, often in a lot of detail. 
Ex. Biography must to some extent delineate characters.
 
Applaud (verb) :
To praise, or express approval for something or someone.
Ex. The audience laughed and applauded.
Ex. Every person stood to applaud his unforgettable act of courage.
 
Assassinate (verb) :
When someone important is assassinated, they are murdered as a political act.
Ex. Would the USA be radically different today if Kennedy had not been assassinated?
questions.source: English and Logical Reasoning Prep Guide

questions.subject: English
questions.examName: English and Logical Reasoning Prep Guide
questions.q:
Choose the word that is most opposite in meaning to the given word:
Garrulous
questions.solution:
Garrulous (adj) :
If you describe someone as garrulous, you mean that they talk a great deal, especially about unimportant things.
Ex. ...a garrulous old woman.
 
Facile (adj) :
If you describe someone's arguments or suggestions as facile, you are criticizing them because their ideas are too simple and indicate a lack of careful, intelligent thinking. = simplistic
Ex. The subject of racism is admittedly too complex for facile summarization.
    
Taciturn (adj) :
A taciturn person does not say very much and can seem unfriendly.
Ex. He is a taciturn man, speaks less most of the times.
 
Gracious (adj) :
If you describe someone, especially someone you think is superior to you, as gracious, you mean that they are very well-mannered and pleasant. = courteous
Ex. She is a lovely and gracious woman. 
    
Plangent (adj) :
A plangent sound is a deep, loud sound, which may be sad. 
Ex. ...plangent violins.
questions.source: English and Logical Reasoning Prep Guide

questions.subject: English
questions.examName: English and Logical Reasoning Prep Guide
questions.q:
Directions: Some words have been given below along with a statement where they have been used in a certain context. You have to find the word from the options which is the most opposite to the given context and mark that as your answer.
PAUCITY: A paucity of urea is worrying farmers for the past few days in several states in north India.
questions.solution:
Paucity: a scarcity or lack of something.
 
Dearth, insufficient and want all are synonyms of paucity.
 
Only surfeit meaning plenty is correct.
 
Hence, option C is correct.
questions.source: English and Logical Reasoning Prep Guide

questions.subject: English
questions.examName: English and Logical Reasoning Prep Guide
questions.q:
Choose the word that is most opposite in meaning to the given word:
Fatuous
questions.solution:
Fatuous (adj):
If you describe a person, action, or remark as fatuous, you think that they are extremely silly, showing a lack of intelligence or thought; idiotic 
Ex: The Chief was left speechless by this fatuous remark.
 
Inane (adj) :
If you describe someone's behaviour or actions as inane, you think they are very silly or stupid; idiotic
Ex. He always had this inane grin...
 
Eccentric (adj) :
If you say that someone is eccentric, you mean that they behave in a strange way, and have habits or opinions that are different from those of most people; odd
Ex: He is an eccentric character who likes wearing a beret and dark glasses.
 
Eclectic (adj) :
An eclectic collection of objects, ideas, or beliefs is wide-ranging and comes from many different sources; diverse
Ex: ...an eclectic collection of paintings, drawings, and prints.
 
Sagacious (adj) :
A sagacious person is intelligent and has the ability to make good decisions.
questions.source: English and Logical Reasoning Prep Guide

questions.subject: English
questions.examName: English and Logical Reasoning Prep Guide
questions.q:
Choose the word that is most opposite in meaning to the given word:
Bellicose
questions.solution:
Bellicose (adj) :
You use bellicose to refer to aggressive actions or behaviour that are likely to start an argument or a fight.

Ingenious (adj) :
Something that is ingenious is very clever and involves new ideas, methods, or equipment.
Ex. A truly ingenious invention.

Amicable (adj) :
When people have an amicable relationship, they are pleasant to each other and solve their problems without quarrelling. 
Ex. The meeting ended on reasonably amicable terms.

Belligerent (adj) :
A belligerent person is hostile and aggressive.
Ex. He was almost back to his belligerent mood of twelve months ago.

Agnostic (adj) :
An agnostic believes that it is not possible to know whether God exists or not.
questions.source: English and Logical Reasoning Prep Guide

questions.subject: English
questions.examName: English and Logical Reasoning Prep Guide
questions.q:
Choose the word that is most opposite in meaning to the given word:
Ignominious
questions.solution:
Ignominious (adj) : If you describe an experience or action as ignominious, you mean it is embarrassing because it shows a great lack of success. = humiliating
 
Ex. Brazil's ignominious defeat against Germany in the semi-final.
 
Sordid (adj) : If you describe someone's behaviour as sordid, you mean that it is immoral or dishonest.
 
Ex. I don't want to hear the sordid details of your relationship with Maya. 
 
Respectable (adj) : Someone or something that is respectable is approved of by society and considered to be morally correct.
 
Ex. He came from a perfectly respectable middle-class family.
 
Ingenuous (adj) : If you describe someone as ingenuous, you mean that they are innocent, trusting, and honest. 

Ex. He seemed too ingenuous for a reporter.
 
Opprobrious (adj) : Opprobrious is a heavy-duty word to describe something taunting or shameful. Opprobrious words criticize in a mean, hurtful way.
questions.source: English and Logical Reasoning Prep Guide

questions.subject: English
questions.examName: English and Logical Reasoning Prep Guide
questions.q:
Choose the word that is most opposite in meaning to the given word:
Beguile
questions.solution:
Beguile (verb) :
1. If something beguiles you, you are charmed and attracted by it.
2. If someone beguiles you into doing something, they trick you into doing it.

Annoy (verb) :
If someone or something annoys you, it takes you fairly angry and impatient.
Ex. It annoyed me that I didn't have to do more reading.

Bamboozle (verb) : 
To bamboozle someone means to confuse them greatly and often trick them.

Belittle (verb) :
If you belittle someone or something, you say or imply that they are unimportant or not very good.
Ex. We mustn't belittle our outstanding achievement. 

Jeopardize (verb) :
To jeopardize a situation or activity means to do something that may destroy it or cause it to fail.
Ex. He has jeopardised the future of his government.
questions.source: English and Logical Reasoning Prep Guide

questions.subject: English
questions.examName: English and Logical Reasoning Prep Guide
questions.q:
Choose the word that is most opposite in meaning to the given word:
Capricious
questions.solution:
Capricious (adj) :
1. Determined by chance or impulse or whim rather than by necessity or reason.
2. Someone who is capricious often changes their mind unexpectedly.
Ex. The Union accused Walesa of being capricious and undemocratic.
 
Caustic (adj) :
A caustic remark is extremely critical, cruel, or bitter. 
Ex. His abrasive wit and caustic comments were an interviewer's nightmare...
 
Predictable (adj) :
If you say that an event is predictable, you mean that it is obvious in advance that it will happen.
Ex. This was a predictable reaction, given the bitter hostility between the two countries...
 
Prescient (adj) :
If you say that someone or something was prescient, you mean that they were able to know or predict what was going to happen in the future. 
 
Cogent (adj) :
A cogent reason, argument, or example is strong and convincing.
Ex. There were perfectly cogent reasons why Julian Cavendish should be told of the Major's impending return.
questions.source: English and Logical Reasoning Prep Guide

questions.subject: English
questions.examName: English and Logical Reasoning Prep Guide
questions.q:
Choose the word that is most opposite in meaning to the given word:
Deleterious
questions.solution:
Deleterious (adj) :
Something that has a deleterious effect on something has a harmful effect on it. 
Ex. Petty crime is having a deleterious effect on community life.
 
Detrimental (adj) :
Something that is detrimental to something else has a harmful or damaging effect on it.
Ex. ...foods suspected of being detrimental to health.
 
Nostalgic (adj) :
Nostalgic things cause you to think affectionately about the past.
Ex. Although we still depict nostalgic snow scenes on Christmas cards, winters are now very much warmer.
Ex. Somehow the place even smelt wonderfully nostalgic.
 
Pragmatic
A pragmatic way of dealing with something is based on practical considerations, rather than theoretical ones. A pragmatic person deals with things in a practical way.
Ex. ...a pragmatic approach to the problems faced by South Indians.
 
Advantageous 
If something is advantageous to you, it is likely to benefit you.
Ex. Free exchange of goods was advantageous to all.
questions.source: English and Logical Reasoning Prep Guide

questions.subject: English
questions.examName: English and Logical Reasoning Prep Guide
questions.q:
Choose the word that is most opposite in meaning to the given word:
Fallible
questions.solution:
Fallible (adj) :
If you say that someone or something is fallible, you mean that they are not perfect and are likely to make mistakes or to fail in what they are doing. 
Ex: They are only human and all too fallible...
 
Faltering (adj) :
A faltering attempt, effort, or movement is uncertain because the person doing it is nervous or weak, or does not really know what to do; hesitant
 
Gormless (adj) :
If you say that someone is gormless, you think that they are stupid because they do not understand things very well. 

Inept (adj) :
If you say that someone is inept, you are criticizing them because they do something with a complete lack of skill.
Ex: He was inept and lacked the intelligence to govern...
 
Unerring (adj) :
If you describe someone's judgment or ability as unerring, you mean that they are always correct and never mistaken.
questions.source: English and Logical Reasoning Prep Guide

questions.subject: English
questions.examName: English and Logical Reasoning Prep Guide
questions.q:
Choose the word that is most opposite in meaning to the given word:
Eloquent
questions.solution:
Eloquent (adj) :
Speech or writing that is eloquent is well expressed and effective in persuading people.
Ex. I heard him make a very eloquent speech at that dinner. 
 
Inarticulate (adj) :
If someone is inarticulate, they are unable to express themselves easily or well in speech.
Ex. Inarticulate and rather shy, he had always dreaded speaking in public...
 
Inadmissible (adj) :
If you say that something that someone says or does is inadmissible, you think that it is totally unacceptable.
Ex. He said the use of force would be inadmissible.
 
Facile (adj) :
If you describe someone's arguments or suggestions as facile, you are criticizing them because their ideas are too simple and indicate a lack of careful, intelligent thinking.
Ex. The subject of racism is admittedly too complex for facile summarization.
 
Flippant (adj) :
If you describe a person or what they say as flippant, you are criticizing them because you think they are not taking something as seriously as they should.
Ex. Don't be flippant, damn it! This is serious!...
questions.source: English and Logical Reasoning Prep Guide

questions.subject: English
questions.examName: English and Logical Reasoning Prep Guide
questions.q:
Choose the word that is most opposite in meaning to the given word:
Candid
questions.solution:
Candid (adj) :
When you are candid about something or with someone, you speak honestly.
Ex. I haven't been completely candid with him.
Ex. ...a candid interview.
 
Disingenuous (adj) :
Someone who is disingenuous is slightly dishonest and insincere in what they say.
Ex. It would be disingenuous to claim that this is great art.
 
Disgruntled (adj) :
If you are disgruntled, you are cross and dissatisfied because things have not happened the way that you wanted them to happen.
Ex. Disgruntled employees recently called for his resignation...
 
Impassive (adj) :
If someone is impassive or their face is impassive, they are not showing any emotion. 
Ex. He searched Hill's impassive face for some indication that he understood...
 
Languorous (adj) :
If you describe an activity as languorous, you mean that it is lazy, relaxed, and not energetic, usually in a pleasant way. 
Ex. ...languorous morning coffees on the terrace.
questions.source: English and Logical Reasoning Prep Guide

questions.subject: English
questions.examName: English and Logical Reasoning Prep Guide
questions.q:
Directions: Some words have been given below along with a statement where they have been used in a certain context. You have to find the word from the options which is the most opposite to the given context and mark that as your answer.
HAPLESS : Hapless farmers are committing suicide after government gave them paltry compensation for acquiring acres of land for a solar power plant.
questions.solution:
Hapless means unfortunate.
 
Clearly, fortunate is the most opposite word here.
 
Hence, option D is correct.
questions.source: English and Logical Reasoning Prep Guide

questions.subject: English
questions.examName: English and Logical Reasoning Prep Guide
questions.q:
Choose the word that is most opposite in meaning to the given word:
Elated
questions.solution:
Elated (adj) :
If you are elated, you are extremely happy and excited because of something that has happened.
Ex. I was elated that my second heart bypass had been successful...
 
Jubilant (adj) :
If you are jubilant, you feel extremely happy because of a success.
Ex. Firdaus was jubilant after making an impressive comeback from a month on the injured list.
 
Impassive (adj) :
If someone is impassive or their face is impassive, they are not showing any emotion.
Ex. He searched Hill's impassive face for some indication that he understood...
 
Imminent (adj) : 
If you say that something is imminent, especially something unpleasant, you mean it is almost certain to happen very soon.
Ex. There appeared no imminent danger...
 
Dejected (adj) :
If you are dejected, you feel miserable or unhappy, especially because you have just been disappointed by something.
Ex. Everyone has days when they feel dejected or down.
questions.source: English and Logical Reasoning Prep Guide

questions.subject: English
questions.examName: English and Logical Reasoning Prep Guide
questions.q:
Choose the word that is most opposite in meaning to the given word:
Effervescent
questions.solution:
Effervescent (adj) :
If you describe someone as effervescent, you mean that they are lively, entertaining, enthusiastic, and exciting.
Ex. ...an effervescent blonde actress...
 
Vivacious (adj) :
If you describe someone as vivacious, you mean that they are lively, exciting, and attractive. 
Ex. She's beautiful, vivacious, and charming.
 
Dull (adj) :
Someone or something that is dull is not very lively or energetic; sluggish
Ex. The body's natural rhythms mean we all feel dull and sleepy between 1 and 3pm.
 
Mediocre (adj) :
If you describe something as mediocre, you mean that it is of average quality but you think it should be better.
Ex. His school record was mediocre.
 
Generous (adj) :
A generous person is friendly, helpful, and willing to see the good qualities in someone or something.
Ex. He was always generous in sharing his enormous knowledge...
Ex. He was generous enough to congratulate his successor on his decision.
questions.source: English and Logical Reasoning Prep Guide

questions.subject: English
questions.examName: English and Logical Reasoning Prep Guide
questions.q:
Directions: Some words have been given below along with a statement where they have been used in a certain context. You have to find the word from the options which is the most opposite to the given context and mark that as your answer.
PECULIAR: People go trekking and wander in the adjacent forest witnessing some of the peculiar flora and fauna of the Himalayas.
questions.solution:
Peculiar: different to what is normal or expected; strange.
 
As per the meaning above, ‘normal’ is most opposite in context here.
 
Hence, option C is correct.
questions.source: English and Logical Reasoning Prep Guide

questions.subject: English
questions.examName: English and Logical Reasoning Prep Guide
questions.q:
Choose the word that is most opposite in meaning to the given word:
Heretic
questions.solution:
Heretic (noun) :
A heretic is someone whose beliefs or actions are considered wrong by most people, because they disagree with beliefs that are generally accepted.
Ex. He was considered a heretic and was ridiculed and ostracized for his ideas.
 
Dissident (noun) :
Dissidents are people who disagree with and criticize their government, especially because it is undemocratic.
Ex. ...political dissidents.
 
Believer (noun) :
A believer is someone who is sure that God exists or that their religion is true.
Ex. I made no secret of the fact that I was not a believer.
 
Offender (noun) : You can refer to someone or something which you think is causing a problem as an offender. = culprit
Ex. The contraceptive pill is the worst offender, but it is not the only drug to deplete the body's vitamin levels.
    
Orthodox (noun) : Orthodox beliefs, methods, or systems are ones which are accepted or used by most people.  = conventional
Ex. Many of these ideas are now being incorporated into orthodox medical treatment.
questions.source: English and Logical Reasoning Prep Guide

questions.subject: English
questions.examName: English and Logical Reasoning Prep Guide
questions.q:
Directions: Some words have been given below along with a statement where they have been used in a certain context. You have to find the word from the options which is the most opposite to the given context and mark that as your answer.
FORTHCOMING: The minister asked its coalition partner to treat regional parties well before striking an alliance for the forthcoming Lok Sabha elections.
questions.solution:
Forthcoming: about to happen or appear/ willing to divulge information/candid.
 
The word that does not fit in here is unavailable which is opposite in meaning to ‘forthcoming’.
 
Hence, option C is correct.
questions.source: English and Logical Reasoning Prep Guide

questions.subject: English
questions.examName: English and Logical Reasoning Prep Guide
questions.q:
Directions: Some words have been given below along with a statement where they have been used in a certain context. You have to find the word from the options which is the most opposite to the given context and mark that as your answer.
IMPETUOUS: Keeping at his impetuous ways, US President Donald Trump created history as he became the first occupant of the White House to shake hands with a North Korean leader, Kim Jong-un.
questions.solution:
Impetuous: acting or done quickly and without thought or care.
 
Options A, B and D are synonyms while bridled means ‘restrained’ and is correct.
 
Hence, option C is correct.
questions.source: English and Logical Reasoning Prep Guide

questions.subject: English
questions.examName: English and Logical Reasoning Prep Guide
questions.q:
Choose the word that is most opposite in meaning to the given word:
Costly
questions.solution:
Costly (Adj.): If you say that something is costly, you mean that it costs a lot of money, often more than you would want to pay. = expensive ≠ inexpensive  

Ex: “In those weather conditions any mistakes could be costly”

Economical (Adj.): Economical means using the minimum amount of time, effort, or language that is necessary.

Ex:  Nano is a very economical little car.
Ex. Our stay at OYO rooms has always been economical.
questions.source: English and Logical Reasoning Prep Guide

questions.subject: English
questions.examName: English and Logical Reasoning Prep Guide
questions.q:
Choose the word that is most opposite in meaning to the given word:
Haggard
questions.solution:
Haggard (adj) :
Someone who looks haggard has a tired expression and shadows under their eyes, especially because they are ill or have not had enough sleep.
Ex. He was pale and a bit haggard.
 
Exuberant (adj) :
If you are exuberant, you are full of energy, excitement, and cheerfulness.
Ex. ...an exuberant young girl who decided to become a screen actress.
 
Vile (adj) :
If you say that someone or something is vile, you mean that they are very unpleasant. = foul
Ex. She was in too vile a mood to work.
 
Emaciated (adj) :
A person or animal that is emaciated is extremely thin and weak because of illness or lack of food.
Ex. ...horrific television pictures of emaciated prisoners.
 
Exquisite (adj) :
Something that is exquisite is extremely beautiful or pleasant, especially in a delicate way.
Ex. Late M.F. Hussain's painting was exquisite.
questions.source: English and Logical Reasoning Prep Guide

questions.subject: English
questions.examName: English and Logical Reasoning Prep Guide
questions.q:
Choose the word that is most opposite in meaning to the given word:
Egalitarian
questions.solution:
Egalitarian (adj) :
Egalitarian means supporting or following the idea that all people are equal and should have the same rights and opportunities.
Ex. I still believe in the notion of an egalitarian society.
 
Bourgeois (adj) :
If you describe people, their way of life, or their attitudes as bourgeois, you disapprove of them because you consider them typical of conventional middle-class people.
Ex. He's accusing them of having a bourgeois and limited vision.
 
Oppressed (adj) :
People who are oppressed are treated cruelly or are prevented from having the same opportunities, freedom, and benefits as others.
Ex. Before they took power, they felt oppressed by the white English speakers who controlled things...
 
Elitist (adj) :
Elitist systems, practices, or ideas favour the most powerful, rich, or talented people within a group, place, or society.
Ex. The legal profession is starting to be less elitist and more representative.
 
Noble (adj) :
Noble means belonging to a high social class and having a title.
Ex. Although he was of noble birth he lived as a poor man.
questions.source: English and Logical Reasoning Prep Guide

questions.subject: English
questions.examName: English and Logical Reasoning Prep Guide
questions.q:
Choose the word that is most opposite in meaning to the given word:
Cacophonous
questions.solution:
Cacophonous (adj) :
If you describe a mixture of sounds as cacophonous, you mean that they are loud and unpleasant.
Ex.  ...the cacophonous beat of pop music.
 
Candid (adj) :
When you are candid about something or with someone, you speak honestly.
Ex. I haven't been completely candid with him.
Ex. ...a candid interview.
 
Clandestine (adj) :
Something that is clandestine is hidden or kept secret, often because it is illegal. 
Ex. ...their clandestine meetings.
 
Inadvertent (adj) :
An inadvertent action is one that you do without realizing what you are doing; unintentional; deliberate
Ex. The government has said it was an inadvertent error.
 
Harmonious (adj) :
1. A harmonious relationship, agreement, or discussion is friendly and peaceful.
Ex. Their harmonious relationship resulted in part from their similar goals.
 
2. Musical notes that are harmonious produce a pleasant sound when played together.
questions.source: English and Logical Reasoning Prep Guide

questions.subject: English
questions.examName: English and Logical Reasoning Prep Guide
questions.q:
Choose the word that is most opposite in meaning to the given word:
Acme
questions.solution:
Acme (noun) : 
The highest point, as of achievement or development.

Pinnacle (noun) :
The highest or culminating point, as of success, power, fame, etc

Abyss (noun) :
1. An abyss is a very deep hole in the ground.
2. If someone is on the edge or brink of an abyss, they are about to enter into a very frightening or threatening situation.

Panacea (noun) :
1. A remedy for all disease or ills; cure-all.
2. An answer or solution for all problems or difficulties.
Ex. His economic philosophy is a good one, but he tries to use it as a panacea.

Precision (noun) :
If you do something with precision, you do it exactly as it should be done.
questions.source: English and Logical Reasoning Prep Guide

questions.subject: English
questions.examName: English and Logical Reasoning Prep Guide
questions.q:
Choose the word that is most opposite in meaning to the given word:
Barbaric
questions.solution:
Barbaric (adj) :
Things that are barbaric, are uncivilized and brutal.
Ex. People have different opinions about hunting - for some, it's a way of life, and for others it's barbaric.

Tempestuous (adj) :
If you describe a relationship or a situation as tempestuous, you mean that very strong and intense emotions, espically anger, are involved.

Civilized (adj) : 
If you describe a person or their behaviour as civilized, you mean that they are polite and reasonable.

Imprudent (adj) :
If you describe someone's behaviour as imprudent, you think it is not sensible or carefully thought out. 
Ex. An imprudent investment

Tedious (adj) : 
If you describe something such as job, task, or situation as tedious, you mean it is boring and rather frustrating.
Ex. Such lists are long and tedious to read.
questions.source: English and Logical Reasoning Prep Guide

questions.subject: English
questions.examName: English and Logical Reasoning Prep Guide
questions.q:
Choose the word that is most opposite in meaning to the given word:
Abase
questions.solution:
Abase (verb) :
To abase something or someone is to humiliate them - no, more than just humiliate them. If you abase another person you are bringing them low, humbling them in a mean, base manner. Not nice at all.

Abhor (verb) :
If you abhor something, it gives you a feeling of complete hatred.

Dignify (verb) : 
To dignify is to grant nobility or respect to something. Dignifying raises the status of things.

Abjure (verb) : 
If you abjure something such as a belief or way of life, you state  publicly that you will give it up or that you reject it.

Vindicate (verb) : 
To clear (someone) of blame or suspicion.
questions.source: English and Logical Reasoning Prep Guide

questions.subject: English
questions.examName: English and Logical Reasoning Prep Guide
questions.q:
Choose the word that is most opposite in meaning to the given word:
Acquiesce
questions.solution:
Acquiesce (verb) :
If you acquiesce in something, you agree to do what someone wants or accept what they do.
Ex. Raj seemed to acquiesce in the decision.

Accede (verb) :
If you accede, it means you agree with someone or give in to his or her wish.
Ex. The king acceded to the Prince's demands for more territory and a larger army.

Accentuate (verb) :
To accentuate something is to emphasize it or make it more noticeable.
Ex. His shaven head accentuates his large round face.

Satiate (verb) :
To satisfy to the full. 
Ex. My brother is a glutton. Even the jumbo tub at the movie theater may not be enough to satiate, or satisfy, his desire.

Demur (verb) :
If you demur, you say that you do not agree with something or will not do something that you have been asked to do.
questions.source: English and Logical Reasoning Prep Guide

questions.subject: English
questions.examName: English and Logical Reasoning Prep Guide
questions.q:
Directions: Some words have been given below along with a statement where they have been used in a certain context. You have to find the word from the options which is the most opposite to the given context and mark that as your answer.
INADVERTENT: The company argued the breach was inadvertent and it was unaware of the court order when the automatically generated email went out.
questions.solution:
Inadvertent: not resulting from or achieved through deliberate planning.
 
‘Deliberate’ means ‘on purpose’ and is most opposite to the context here.
 
Hence, option B is correct.
questions.source: English and Logical Reasoning Prep Guide

questions.subject: English
questions.examName: English and Logical Reasoning Prep Guide
questions.q:
Choose the word that is most opposite in meaning to the given word:
Abhor
questions.solution:
Abhor (verb) :
If you abhor something, it gives you a feeling of complete hatred.

Loathe (verb) : 
If you loathe something or someone, you dislike them very much.

Admire (verb) :
If you admire someone or something, you like and respect them very much.

Abdicate (verb) : 
Sometimes, someone in power might decide to give up that power and step down from his or her position. When they do that, they abdicate their authority, giving up all duties and perks of the job.

Excoriate (verb) : 
To excoriate a person or organization means to criticize them severely, usually in public.
questions.source: English and Logical Reasoning Prep Guide

questions.subject: English
questions.examName: English and Logical Reasoning Prep Guide
questions.q:
Directions: Some words have been given below along with a statement where they have been used in a certain context. You have to find the word from the options which is the most opposite to the given context and mark that as your answer.
DEVOID: Devoid of commercial development, the beaches at the Murud coast are practically deserted and make for a relaxed outing.
questions.solution:
Devoid means lacking/ incomplete.
 
As per the meaning above, ‘complete’ is the most opposite here while options A, B and C are synonyms.
 
Hence, option D is correct.
questions.source: English and Logical Reasoning Prep Guide

questions.subject: English
questions.examName: English and Logical Reasoning Prep Guide
questions.q:
Choose the word that is most opposite in meaning to the given word:
Dauntless
questions.solution:
Dauntless (adj) :
A dauntless person is brave and confident and not easily frightened; resolute
Ex. ...their dauntless courage.
 
Mutinous (adj) :
If someone is mutinous, they are strongly dissatisfied with a person in authority and are likely to stop obeying them.
Ex. His own army, stung by defeats, is mutinous.
 
Intrepid (adj) :
An intrepid person acts in a brave way; fearless
Ex. ...an intrepid space traveller.
 
Intriguing (adj) :
If you describe something as intriguing, you mean that it is interesting or strange; fascinating
Ex. This intriguing book is both thoughtful and informative.
 
Timid (adj) :
Timid people are shy, nervous, and have no courage or confidence in themselves; Not confident
Ex. A timid child, Riya had learned obedience at an early age.
questions.source: English and Logical Reasoning Prep Guide

questions.subject: English
questions.examName: English and Logical Reasoning Prep Guide
questions.q:
Choose the word that is most opposite in meaning to the given word:
Hideous
questions.solution:
Hideous (adj) : 
If you say that someone or something is hideous, you mean that they are very ugly or unattractive.
Ex. She saw a hideous face at the window and screamed.

Abominable (adj) :
Something that is abominable is very unpleasant or bad.
Ex. The President described the killings as an abominable crime.

Grotesque (adj) :
You say that something is grotesque when it is so unnatural, unpleasant, and exaggerated that it upsets or shocks you.
Ex. Tthe grotesque disparities between the wealthy few and nearly everyone else.

Alluring (adj) :
Someone or something that is alluring is very attractive. = fascinating
Ex.  The most alluring city in UAE is Dubai.

Macabre (adj) :
You describe something such as an event or story as macabre when it is strange and horrible or upsetting, usually because it involves death or injury.
Ex. Police have made a macabre discovery.
questions.source: English and Logical Reasoning Prep Guide

questions.subject: English
questions.examName: English and Logical Reasoning Prep Guide
questions.q:
Choose the word that is most opposite in meaning to the given word:
Haughty
questions.solution:
Haughty (adj) : 
Arrogantly superior and disdainful.
Ex. He spoke in a haughty tone.
 
Pompous (adj) :
If you describe someone as pompous, you mean that they behave or speak in a very serious way because they think they are more important than they really are.
Ex. He was somewhat pompous and had a high opinion of his own capabilities.
 
Sullen (adj) :
Someone who is sullen is bad-tempered and does not speak much.
Ex. The offenders lapsed into a sullen silence.
 
Spurious (adj) :
Something that is spurious seems to be genuine, but is false.
Ex. He was arrested in 1979 on spurious corruption charges.
 
Modest (adj) :
Having or showing a modest or low estimate of one's importance.
Ex. "I felt very humble when meeting her"
questions.source: English and Logical Reasoning Prep Guide

questions.subject: English
questions.examName: English and Logical Reasoning Prep Guide
questions.q:
Choose the word that is most opposite in meaning to the given word:
Calamitous
questions.solution:
Calamitous (adj) :
If you describe an event or situation as calamitous, you mean it is very unfortunate or serious; disastrous
Ex. ...the calamitous state of the country.
 
Fortunate (adj) :
If you say that someone or something is fortunate, you mean that they are lucky.
 
Haggard (adj) :
Someone who looks haggard has a tired expression and shadows under their eyes, especially because they are ill or have not had enough sleep.
Ex. He was pale and a bit haggard...
 
Ambivalent (adj) :
If you say that someone is ambivalent about something, they seem to be uncertain whether they really want it, or whether they really approve of it; unsure
Ex. She remained ambivalent about her marriage...
 
Cantankerous (adj) :
Someone who is cantankerous is always finding things to argue or complain about.
Ex. ...a cantankerous old man.
questions.source: English and Logical Reasoning Prep Guide

questions.subject: English
questions.examName: English and Logical Reasoning Prep Guide
questions.q:
Choose the word that is most opposite in meaning to the given word:
Bemoan
questions.solution:
Bemoan (verb) :
If you bemoan something, you express sorrow or dissatisfaction about it. 
Ex. Universities and other research establishments bemoan their lack of funds.

Exult (verb) :
If you exult in a triumph or success that you have had, you feel and show great happiness and pleasure because of it.
Ex. I exulted and wept for joy.

Mourn (verb) :
1. If you mourn someone who has died or mourn for them, you are very sad that they have died and show your sorrow in the way that you behave.
2. If you mourn something or mourn for it, you regret that you no longer have it and show your regret in the way that you behave.

Agitate (verb) :
If something agitates you, it worries you and makes you unable to think clearly and calmly. 

Extol (verb) :
If you extol something or someone, you praise them enthusiastically.
questions.source: English and Logical Reasoning Prep Guide

questions.subject: English
questions.examName: English and Logical Reasoning Prep Guide
questions.q:
Choose the word that is most opposite in meaning to the given word:
Abundant
questions.solution:
Abundant (adj) : 
We use the word abundant to describe something that exists in large amounts that are more than what's needed.

Scarce (adj) :
If something is scarce, there isn't much of it around.

Acrimonious (adj) : 
Acrimonious words or  quarrels are bitter and angry.

Ingenuous (adj) :
If you describe someone as ingenuous, you mean that they are innocent, trusting and honest.

Heavenly (adj) : 
Something that is heavenly is very pleasant and enjoyable.
questions.source: English and Logical Reasoning Prep Guide

questions.subject: English
questions.examName: English and Logical Reasoning Prep Guide
questions.q:
Choose the word that is most opposite in meaning to the given word:
Gaudy
questions.solution:
Gaudy (adj) : Something that's gaudy is extravagantly bright or showy, typically so as to be tasteless.
Ex. ...her gaudy orange-and-purple floral hat.
 
Ostentatious (adj) : If you describe something as ostentatious, you disapprove of it because it is expensive and is intended to impress people.
Ex. ...an ostentatious wedding reception.
 
Sumptuous (adj): Something that is sumptuous is grand and obviously very expensive.
Ex. She produces elegant wedding gowns in a variety of sumptuous fabrics.
 
Modest (adj) : Free from showiness or ostentation; unpretentious.
Ex. Her modest dress attracted everyone's attention at the party.
 
Blatant (adj) : of bad behaviour) done openly and unashamedly.
Ex. "blatant lies"
questions.source: English and Logical Reasoning Prep Guide

questions.subject: English
questions.examName: English and Logical Reasoning Prep Guide
questions.q:
Choose the word that is most opposite in meaning to the given word:
Hapless
questions.solution:
Hapless (adj) :
(especially of a person) unfortunate.
Ex. "the hapless victims of the disaster"
 
Discerning (adj) :
Having or showing good judgement.
Ex. " ... discerning customers"
 
Doomed (adj) :
Someone or something that is doomed is certain to fail or be destroyed.
Ex. I used to pour time and energy into projects that were doomed from the start.
 
Fortunate (adj) :
If you say that someone or something is fortunate, you mean that they are lucky.
Ex. He was extremely fortunate to survive...
 
Imitative (adj) :
People and animals who are imitative copy others' behaviour.
Ex. Babies of eight to twelve months are generally highly imitative.
questions.source: English and Logical Reasoning Prep Guide

questions.subject: English
questions.examName: English and Logical Reasoning Prep Guide
questions.q:
Choose the word that is most opposite in meaning to the given word:
Defunct
questions.solution:
Defunct (adj) :
If something is defunct, it no longer exists or has stopped functioning or operating.
Ex. ...the leader of the now defunct Social Democratic Party.
 
Absurd (adj) :
If you say that something is absurd, you are criticizing it because you think that it is ridiculous or that it does not make sense; ridiculous
Ex. It is absurd to be discussing compulsory redundancy policies for teachers...
Ex. I've known clients of mine go to absurd lengths, just to avoid paying me a few pounds...
 
Pliant (adj) :
A pliant person can be easily influenced and controlled by other people; compliant
Ex. She's proud and stubborn, you know, under that pliant exterior.
    
Live (adj) :
Live animals or plants are alive, rather than being dead or artificial.
Ex. ...a protest against the company's tests on live animals.
Ex. ...baskets of live chickens.    
 
Virulent (adj) :
Virulent feelings or actions are extremely bitter and hostile.
Ex. Now he faces virulent attacks from the Italian media.
questions.source: English and Logical Reasoning Prep Guide

questions.subject: English
questions.examName: English and Logical Reasoning Prep Guide
questions.q:
Choose the word that is most opposite in meaning to the given word:
Illustrious
questions.solution:
Illustrious (adj) : If you describe someone as an illustrious person, you mean that they are extremely well known because they have a high position in society or they have done something impressive. = distinguished
 
Ex. Sachin Tendulkar had a long and illustrious career.
   
Conspicuous (adj) : If someone or something is conspicuous, people can see or notice them very easily.
 
Ex. The most conspicuous way in which the old politics is changing is in the growing use of referendums.
 
Venerable (adj) : A venerable person deserves respect because they are old and wise.
 
Ex. ...a venerable old man with white hair.
 
Unknown (adj) : An unknown person is not famous or publicly recognized.

Ex. He was an unknown writer.
Ex. ...a popular environment where both established and unknown artists can meet, talk and drink.
 
Resplendent (adj) : If you describe someone or something as resplendent, you mean that their appearance is very impressive and expensive-looking.
 
Ex. Aliya, resplendent in royal blue velvet, was hovering beside the table.
questions.source: English and Logical Reasoning Prep Guide

questions.subject: English
questions.examName: English and Logical Reasoning Prep Guide
questions.q:
Choose the word that is most opposite in meaning to the given word:
Ebullient
questions.solution:
Ebullient (adj) :
If you describe someone as ebullient, you mean that they are lively and full of enthusiasm or excitement about something. 
Ex. ...the ebullient Russian President.
 
Eccentric (adj) :
If you say that someone is eccentric, you mean that they behave in a strange way, and have habits or opinions that are different from those of most people; odd
Ex. He is an eccentric character who likes wearing a beret and dark glasses.
 
Sanguine (adj) :
If you are sanguine about something, you are cheerful and confident that things will happen in the way you want them to.
Ex. He's remarkably sanguine about the problems involved...
 
Spiritless (adj) :
If someone is spiritless, they lack energy, courage, and liveliness.
Ex. They were too spiritless even to resist.
 
Macabre (adj) :
You describe something such as an event or story as macabre when it is strange and horrible or upsetting, usually because it involves death or injury.
Ex. Police have made a macabre discovery...
questions.source: English and Logical Reasoning Prep Guide

questions.subject: English
questions.examName: English and Logical Reasoning Prep Guide
questions.q:
Choose the word that is most opposite in meaning to the given word:
Facetious
questions.solution:
Facetious (adj) :
If you say that someone is being facetious, you are criticizing them because they are making humorous remarks or saying things that they do not mean in a situation where they ought to be serious.
 
Sacrastic (adj) :
Someone who is sarcastic says or does the opposite of what they really mean in order to mock or insult someone.
 
Sanctimonious (adj) :
If you say that someone is sanctimonious, you disapprove of them because you think that they are trying to appear morally better than other people.
 
Humourless (adj) :
Someone or something that is solemn is very serious rather than cheerful or humorous.
Ex: His solemn little face broke into smiles...
 
Flippant (adj) :
If you describe a person or what they say as flippant, you are criticizing them because you think they are not taking something as seriously as they should.
questions.source: English and Logical Reasoning Prep Guide

questions.subject: English
questions.examName: English and Logical Reasoning Prep Guide
questions.q:
Directions: Some words have been given below along with a statement where they have been used in a certain context. You have to find the word from the options which is the most opposite to the given context and mark that as your answer.
ANTAGONIZE: Canada has decided to not antagonize India in spite of its dismal human rights record.
questions.solution:
Antagonize: cause (someone) to become hostile.
 
Placate means to calm/pacify/soothe something/someone. This is the most opposite in meaning. The rest are synonyms of antagonize.
 
Hence, option A is correct.
questions.source: English and Logical Reasoning Prep Guide

questions.subject: English
questions.examName: English and Logical Reasoning Prep Guide
questions.q:
Directions: Some words have been given below along with a statement where they have been used in a certain context. You have to find the word from the options which is the most opposite to the given context and mark that as your answer.
VAGUE: The negotiator was vague and did not reveal much about the meeting set to take place the next day.
questions.solution:
Vague: Unclear/indistinct. Options B, C and D are synonyms of vague.
 
Explicit means clear/overt/open/plain and is most opposite in meaning.
 
Hence, option A is correct.
questions.source: English and Logical Reasoning Prep Guide

questions.subject: English
questions.examName: English and Logical Reasoning Prep Guide
questions.q:
Choose the word that is most opposite in meaning to the given word:
Facile
questions.solution:
Facile (adj) :
If someone does something easily, or shows ease, it is described as facile in a good way, but if someone takes the easy way out and shows a lack of thought or care, it is facile in a bad way.
 
Profound (adj) :
A profound idea, work, or person shows great intellectual depth and understanding.
Ex: This is a book full of profound, original and challenging insights.
 
Superficial (adj) :
If you describe someone as superficial, you disapprove of them because they do not think deeply, and have little understanding of anything serious or important.
 
Subliminal (adj) :
Subliminal influences or messages affect your mind without you being aware of it.
Ex: Colour has a profound, though often subliminal influence on our senses and moods.
 
Bucolic (adj) :
Bucolic means relating to the countryside.
questions.source: English and Logical Reasoning Prep Guide
